So our article generation workflow, this is inside Atlas, which is our in-house platform that was built by our engineering team.
And every client has like a workspace like this.
And this is where, Robert, if you remember like the artifacts that you reviewed, this is where they live.
And they influence the workflow at different stages.
And we can edit these at any time.
So.
So whenever we, for example, if update the writing guidelines, we're going to go in here and change them so that the next batch of articles get the new writing guidelines.
And then we have like the pipelines here.
We have different pipelines.
So one of them is for just generating assignments.
We have an article refresh pipeline.
I know we're not doing refreshes for you yet, but we do have that set up.
Specifically, article generation workflow, like it's split into different chunks.
And every stage, almost every stage involves human review.
So the first stage is we enter a topic in here.
This can be a keyword or it can be an assignment title.
And then that's used to create an assignment brief, which is very similar to what a human writer would get.
For example, if you're a freelancer working with a client, they would send you something like this.
So who's the target audience?
What is the SEO data?
Like the keyword analysis?
the keyword analysis?

This is generated, we have an API hooked up to SEMrush, so it's pulling data directly from SEMrush regarding like how many monthly searches there are, what's like the content type, is it supposed to be a listicle, so just to help us meet the search intent and increase like our chances of ranking higher in search.

And this stage also includes a proposed content structure, which is the first version of the outline, and we do a lot of changes even at this stage, because we want to influence the outline that's going to be generated later.

At this stage, might add, for example, if you have any case studies from you, we'll add it in here, have any quotes from your customers or quotes from subject matter experts, we can add them in here, and they're going to be added to the outline, like they're not going to be, there's not going to be any hallucinations, because the proposed content structure here is like directly transported.

And then the next stage is research, which is, this step does like a really deep research using perplexity, and it takes the information from the assignment brief, it does the research, and then it creates a very in-depth research doc, which the workflow references as it's generating the article.
So it's not just like, it's not randomly pulling information from the internet, it's taken via an API call.
After this stage, we move to the outline, and this is another step where we have heavy manual editing.
It creates a standard outline, it contains like every section contains a word count, it sort of contains all the different points that are going to be included in the blog post.
And at this stage, we might remove section.
We might add sections, like if we feel like a section is too long, we're going to condense it.
So this is also another part where we are heavily involved.
Once we're happy with the outline, we will approve it, and then it goes to the article draft process.
At this stage, the writing guidelines get pulled in, so the tone and the voice that you like is reflected in the final content.
After the article draft, we run a fact checker, and then we also add internal links to the blog.
And usually after we add the internal links, that's when we will do a very deep read-through of the content to check the external links, make sure there aren't any hallucinations.
We will remove, like we will make line edits basically like any other editor.
if if if if can if we we can we we
And, you know, once we're happy with that draft, we're going to generate the SEO meta tags for the meta description and the meta title.
A lot of times we will also, like, go in and change this, especially if it's too long for mobile.
But this is what the process looks like from start to finish.
And then we basically just import it into a Google Doc and share it with you.

I just wanted to flag something really quickly when it comes to LLMs and optimizers.
I think for them, really, like, for a very long time in SEO, it used to be a best practice that you will go after keywords that have a good search volume, monthly search volume.
And that makes sense.
With LLMs, however, you want to, especially if you're not, like, this super huge brand, it's a good idea to go after what's called, like, zero volume keywords.
So the keywords have very little content on the top.
That's That's good.
you.
Like on the front page of Google, but people are looking for it.

But a super niche topic related to maybe AI and engineering, perplexity is way more likely to pull up your blog and serve it as a resource.
I've seen this happen with a different client of mine who were, they were in the dental industry, they're in the dental industry.

And they created a bunch of blogs for zero volume searches, and it allowed them to get really good spots in LLMs and got a lot of referral traffic because there's just nothing else on that topic out there.
